retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Five-year survival of hepatocellular carcinoma is 18% and second to pancreatic cancer. The prognosis of patients with HCC is dependent on the tumor size, differentiation or grade of the tumor on histopathology, severity of the underlying liver disease, presence or absence of metastases, and tumor extension to adjacent structures. HCC with high levels of alpha-fetoprotein is associated with poorly differentiated HCC and confers a poor prognosis. [65] Patients with hepatitis B virus-related HCC and positive serum hepatitis B e antigen(HBeAg) are associated with poor prognosis and higher recurrence of HCC. [66] [67] High levels of serum hepatitis B virus DNA are associated with a higher risk for HCC and recurrence. [68] [69] Diabetes mellitus is a risk factor for the development of HCC and also associated with a poor prognosis. [70]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatocellular carcinoma is a malignant tumor of the hepatocytes. Cytological features depend on the differentiation of hepatocytes. From well-differentiated to poorly differentiated HCC. The trabecular architectural pattern is the commonest. Other patterns include pseudoacinar (acinar with proteinaceous material), compact and sarcomatoid. Histology may show variation, according to the differentiation of the tissue: Well-Differentiated: cells smaller than normal, minimal nuclear atypia, nuclear density two times of normal liver Moderately Differentiated: larger tumor cells with more eosinophilic cytoplasm, pseudoglands, distinct nucleoli, bile, and giant tumor cells Poorly Differentiated: large tumor cells with hyperchromatic nuclei prominent pleomorphism may have spindle cell or small-cell areas

retrieved_docs.title: Fibrolamellar Hepatocellular Carcinoma -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Successful surgical treatment is associated with a better overall outcome. However, if there is a locoregional or distant disease, there is a negative impact on outcomes [30] [31] . As expected, survival decreases as the number of disease site involvement increases. [32] If patients have findings of conventional HCC along with FL-HCC, which can rarely occur, there is a negative impact on prognosis. [6] The overall prognosis is superior to conventional HCC in the setting of underlying cirrhosis when compared to patients with a non-cirrhotic liver. [33] There is a statistically significant difference in the survival of patients diagnosed with FL-HCC compared to those with conventional HCC. Factors correlated with improved outcomes include disease confined to the liver, completed removal of the tumor at surgery, and treatment with different modalities as described above. [1] [34] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Resection
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with Child–Turcotte–Pugh A and without clinically significant portal hypertension have favorable surgical resection outcomes. Patients with small HCC (tumors less than 5 cm) and Child-Pugh A have survival rates of 70% and 35% at 5 and 10 years, respectively, and recurrence-free survival rates of 36% and 22%. [46] Predictors of HCC recurrence after surgical resection include micro-and macrovascular invasion, tumor differentiation, and the presence of satellite nodules. Five years risk of recurrence is up to 70%. [47] Adjuvant therapies have not been shown to reduce the risk of recurrence. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnosis of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) includes the following: Cholangiocarcinoma Fibrous nodular hyperplasia Dysplastic/Regenerative nodules in cirrhosis Hepatic adenoma Primary hepatic lymphoma Cirrhosis Metastatic cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Fibrolamellar Hepatocellular Carcinoma -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: These patients benefit from a treatment approach integrating different clinical teams familiar with treating complex malignancies with an evidence-based approach in a higher-level care center with the resources necessary to manage these patients. [35] [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: The BCLC offers the most prognostic information, including patients' performance status, liver burden, and liver function. [63] [64] The system stratifies patients with HCC in one of five stages. Substrata of 0, A, B, C, and D providing treatment recommendations depending on the stage. Tumor burden is assessed according to the size and number of nodules, extrahepatic spread, or presence or absence of macrovascular tumor invasion. The liver function test is assessed with Child–Turcotte–Pugh score, Model for End-Stage Liver Disease, and albumin–bilirubin grade.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: MRI: T1-weighted images may be isointense to hyperintense depending on the degree of fibrosis, fat, and necrosis. Hyperintense images on T1 are mostly well-differentiated tumors and appear as isointense on T2 images. Poorly or moderately differentiated tumors appear as isointense on T1 images and hyperintense on T2 images. Contrast MRI has a sensitivity of 77%-90% and a specificity of 84-97%.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatoblastoma -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognosis is based on numerous factors including age of diagnosis, PRETEXT group, metastases, alfa fetal protein (AFP) levels, histologic subtype, completeness of resection, and clinical stage of the disease.  With certainty, the well-differentiated fetal subtype is associated with a better prognosis compared with small cell undifferentiated and macrotrabecular, which are linked to unfavorable prognosis. [13] Of note, the histologic subtype is only prognostically valuable before treatment with chemotherapy. Alfa-fetoprotein is typically high upon diagnosis, but a significant drop after neo-adjuvant chemotherapy portends a better response to treatment. Younger age of diagnosis has historically been a poor prognostic factor; however, recent studies have called this into question, providing evidence that these younger patients do just as well as older children. [14] Specifically, children younger than 1 year of age have a better prognosis and children greater than 6 years of age have a worse prognosis. [2] Tumor presence at the resection margin, multifocality, and metastases have been shown to be poor prognostic factors. Beta-catenin expression has been shown to be associated with a lower period of event-free survival, while EpCAM expression has been associated with higher tumor viability and a poorer response to neo-adjuvant chemotherapy. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Adenoma -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Complete HCA resolution is unlikely in most patients. About 25% of women will continue to have RUQ pain, with hemorrhage potentially occurring in up to 30% to 45% of women. Bleeding may occur within the lesion or peritoneum. The larger the lesion, the higher the risk of hemorrhage. Pregnancy has been associated with HCA enlargement and increased rupture risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of hepatocellular carcinoma depends on both tumor burden and liver dysfunction. Tumor-node-metastasis (TNM) does not account for the degree of performance status and liver dysfunction. The most widely staging system is the Barcelona clinic liver cancer (BCLC). Among other staging systems include the Chinese University Prognostic Index, Japan Integrated Staging, Cancer of the Liver Italian Program.

retrieved_docs.title: Fibrolamellar Hepatocellular Carcinoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Primary treatment of this tumor involves a treatment strategy that aims at a cure. When possible, complete resection is the preferred treatment, which appears to favor the outcome. [15] However, a high percentage of patients (greater than two-thirds) eventually show recurrent disease. [15] A good portion of these patients have locoregional diagnosis during the surgical treatment, and these additional sites of disease are also treated along with the primary tumor resection, which has shown to have a better prognostic value. [16] These tumors are often very aggressive in their behavior, and a failed surgical attempt at resection, the necessity of second surgical intervention, and finally, hepatic transplantation are often encountered. These additional interventions can hurt the outcome. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Cancer(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Very early stage (0) and early stage (A) may be considered for resection or even liver transplant with a curative rate of 30% and 5-year survival 40% to 70%. After a complete resection for hepatocellular carcinoma, based upon the 2010 TNM (tumor, node, metastasis) staging system, five-year survival rates are Stage I (55%), Stage II (37%) and Stage III (16%). Patient with advanced disease may benefit from prognostic survival evaluation by calculating the Cancer of the Liver Italian Program (CLIP) score which sums points of each of the sub-scores (Child-Pugh stage, Tumor morphology uni-nodular versus multi-nodular and extension less than or greater than 50%, AFP less than or greater than 400, and portal vein thrombosis present or absent) with a mOS of 36, 22, 9, 7, and 3 months for patients in categories 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 to 6, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Fibrolamellar Hepatocellular Carcinoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of this disease includes: Focal nodular hyperplasia Giant cavernous hemangioma Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Adenoma -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for patients with hepatocellular adenoma remains poorly established. Oral contraceptive discontinuation may result in the regression or resolution of some lesions. As previously stated, about 27% of HCAs may rupture, which has a 5% to 10% mortality risk. The risk of HCA's malignant transformation is 4.2%. [23] The risk of malignancy generally persists even after discontinuing oral contraceptives.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Resection
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with Barcelona-clinic liver cancer (BCLC) classification of very early (0) and early-stage (A) are ideal candidates for surgical resection. Very early (0) stage has preserved liver function, European cooperative oncologic group performance status (ECOG-PS) score of 0, and solitary nodule of 2 cm. For patients with early-stage (A) with preserved liver function and ECOG-PS score of 0 and with a solitary nodule of more than 2 cm are appropriate surgical resection candidates. [45]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2676
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell carcinoma), which is broadly related to مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ (jaundiced liver disease). This connection indicates that hepatocellular carcinoma often presents within the spectrum of liver diseases that cause jaundice, highlighting the importance of recognizing jaundice as a potential clinical clue to underlying malignant liver pathology. Understanding this relationship aids clinicians in early detection, accurate diagnosis, and appropriate management of patients with liver‑related malignancies and associated jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: cancer; other and unspecified primary and سرطان الغدة الدرقية are both classified as subtypes of ورم صماوي خبيث, which denotes a malignant tumor. This relationship indicates that these specific cancers fall under the broader category of malignant tumors, sharing the characteristic of uncontrolled, invasive cell growth. Recognizing them as forms of ورم صماوي خبيث helps guide diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1151
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد) is classified as liver inflammation. It is clinically associated with both alcohol‑abstinence syndrome (متلازمة الامتناع عن تعاطي الكحول) and hepatocellular carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكبدية). Hepatocellular carcinoma is a manifestation of cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct and also has a broader relationship with cholestatic liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ). These interconnections highlight the role of hepatitis in the development of liver cancer and related hepatic disorders, emphasizing the importance of monitoring and managing hepatitis to prevent progression to malignancy and other liver complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1206
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct is a malignant condition that manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell cancer). Hepatitis (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد), which is classified as liver inflammation, is clinically associated with سرطان الخلايا الكبدية, indicating that patients with hepatitis may have an increased risk of developing this type of liver cancer. Moreover, سرطان الخلايا الكبدية has a broader relationship with مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ, a jaundiced liver disease, suggesting that liver cell cancer can be part of or contribute to the clinical spectrum of jaundice‑related hepatic disorders. Recognizing the interconnections among cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct, hepatitis, and مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ is essential for comprehensive diagnosis and management of hepatic pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: and hepatocellular carcinoma. Hepatocellular carcinoma represents a manifestation of cancer of the liver and intra‑hepatic bile ducts, and it is further related to cholestatic liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ), illustrating a spectrum from malignant to non‑malignant hepatic disorders. Diagnostic and therapeutic procedures targeting the liver include liver biopsy (خزعة الكبد), performed on partially excised hepatic tissue, and ultrasound examination of the liver (فحص الكبد بالموجات فوق الصوتية), which is a child procedure of diagnostic ultrasound of the gastrointestinal tract. The liver also serves as an indirect site for various non‑gastrointestinal therapeutic interventions. Collectively, these interrelated concepts demonstrate the anatomical and functional integration of the gallbladder, liver, and biliary system; the utility of ultrasonographic techniques for biliary and hepatic pathology; the role of substances as causative agents for renal and hepatic toxicities; and the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 643
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (hepatocellular carcinoma). Hepatitis (اِلْتِهَبُ الكَبِد) is clinically associated with سرطان الخلايا الكبدية, indicating that chronic liver inflammation can predispose to hepatocellular carcinoma. Alcohol dependence (الاعتماد على الكحول) is clinically associated with اِلْتِهَبُ الكَبِد, highlighting alcohol use as a risk factor for liver inflammation. سرطان الخلايا الكبدية also has a broader relationship with مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ, suggesting that hepatocellular carcinoma may be linked to cholestatic or jaundiced liver disease. The term hepatitis classifies اِلْتِهَبُ الكَبِد, confirming the equivalence of the English and Arabic nomenclature. Understanding these interconnections is important for patient care, as alcohol dependence can lead to hepatitis, which in turn increases the risk of hepatocellular carcinoma and may be associated with broader cholestatic liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: carcinomas under the broader category of primary malignant neoplasms. Collectively, these relationships delineate a structured view of digestive system cancers, especially pancreatic malignancies, their metastatic characteristics, and their placement within a comprehensive neoplasm classification system that spans multiple organ sites and includes both well‑defined and uncertain neoplastic entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2517
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a type of اضطراب صماوي, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of endocrine disorders. By recognizing thyroid cancer as an endocrine disorder, clinicians understand that the disease originates in the hormone‑producing thyroid gland and shares pathophysiological features with other conditions affecting the endocrine system. This classification guides diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment within the context of endocrine pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 248
retrieved_docs.content: withdrawal syndrome and hepatocellular carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكبدية), the latter representing cancer of the liver and intra‑hepatic bile ducts and also linked to yellow liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ). Liver biopsy (خزعة الكبد) is a partially excised anatomical procedure classified under biopsy of liver, and the liver can serve as an indirect site for other non‑gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures. Together, these interconnections highlight an integrated clinical framework that combines abdominal and retroperitoneal anatomy, diagnostic and therapeutic procedures, toxic etiologies affecting renal and hepatic systems, and comprehensive detoxification and rehabilitation strategies for substance‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the disease has spread beyond its primary site. In the context of سرطانة العظم, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component that drives the malignancy. Together, the presence of malignant tumor cells and the detection of metastatic lesions provide critical information for diagnosing, staging, and managing سرطانة العظم, guiding therapeutic decisions and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 240
retrieved_docs.content: similarly defined as a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation. In addition, an inverse‑is‑a relationship links the entity “سرطانة توتية” to the primary malignant tumor morphology (“ورم خبيث أولي”), further emphasizing its classification as a type of primary malignant neoplasm. Together, these relationships illustrate how various primary malignant neoplasms across different organs are unified by common pathological processes, shared morphological descriptors, and hierarchical links to broader cancer concepts, aiding in consistent classification and clinical understanding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1262
retrieved_docs.content: The liver, referred to as “játra” in Czech, is the anatomical site for a range of diagnostic procedures, diseases, and therapeutic interventions. The procedure known in English as “biopsy of liver” is classified by the Arabic term “خزعة الكبد,” and this partially excised‑anatomy procedure is performed on the liver (“játra”). Hepatitis, identified in Arabic as “اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد,” also localizes to the liver and is clinically associated with hepatocellular carcinoma (“سرطان الخلايا الكبدية”). Hepatocellular carcinoma represents a manifestation of “cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct,” and it has a broader relationship to the disease category “مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ.” In addition to these liver‑specific conditions, the liver serves as an indirect site for other non‑gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1074
retrieved_docs.content: Citalopram is an antidepressant medication; it is not treated by alcohol dependence, and the suggestion that alcohol dependence could serve as therapy for citalopram is inaccurate. Alcohol dependence, however, is clinically associated with inflammation of the liver (hepatitis), and this relationship can exacerbate liver pathology. Hepatitis is classified as inflammation of the liver (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد) and represents a key clinical condition that links several liver‑related diseases. Hepatitis is also clinically associated with hepatocellular carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكبدية), a specific manifestation of cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile ducts. In turn, hepatocellular carcinoma has a broader relationship with cholestatic liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ), reflecting its place within the spectrum of serious liver disorders. Understanding these interconnections—alcohol dependence contributing to hepatitis, hepatitis predisposing to hepatocellular carcinoma, and hepatocellular carcinoma being part of broader cholestatic liver disease—helps clinicians assess risk factors, monitor disease progression, and devise comprehensive management strategies for patients with liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). Liver cirrhosis, in turn, is clinically associated with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Recognizing that patients with alcoholic cirrhosis develop the broader condition of liver cirrhosis helps clinicians anticipate the high risk of variceal hemorrhage, prompting appropriate screening, monitoring, and preventive interventions to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1905
retrieved_docs.content: Benign tumor cells are not regarded as a finding of pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي), indicating that their presence does not support a diagnosis of this malignancy. In contrast, tumor cells whose nature is uncertain—whether benign or malignant—are considered abnormal cells of pancreatic cancer, reflecting their potential relevance to the disease process. Recognizing the difference between clearly benign cells and cells of indeterminate behavior is essential for accurate pathological assessment and appropriate patient management in the context of pancreatic cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, represent the abnormal cellular component of السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer). Their ambiguous nature underscores the diagnostic challenge in distinguishing benign from malignant lesions within the pancreas, influencing treatment decisions and overall patient management.
